one_line_takeaway: A qualitative survey that organizes the Blockchain-based Federated Learning literature into a 3-class architectural taxonomy and a generic 6-layer system stack, arguing blockchain removes FL's single-point-of-failure aggregator and adds traceable trust plus native incentives -- but it contributes no experiments or measurements of its own.
key_finding: The paper's synthesizing claim is that blockchain and FL are complementary: blockchain contributes decentralization, immutability/traceability, consensus-based trust, and native token incentive mechanisms, while FL contributes privacy-preserving collaborative training; together they remove the single-point-of-failure aggregator and enable accountable, incentive-aligned, tamper-evident decentralized learning. It argues no single consensus mechanism dominates -- choice trades off decentralization vs. security vs. scalability -- and that FL-specific consensus variants (Proof of Training Quality, Proof of Federated Training/FL) can replace PoW's 'meaningless' computation with useful model-quality verification. It identifies scalability (via off-chain computation, sidechains, and layer-2 like state channels/Plasma) and privacy-preserving cryptography as the principal unresolved bottlenecks.
core_contribution: This is a survey (no new system or experiments of its own). Its self-declared contribution is a synthesis of the Blockchain-based Federated Learning (BCFL) literature into two organizing frameworks: (1) a taxonomy of BCFL architectures along three operational dynamics -- fully coupled (clients act as both training and blockchain nodes), flexibly coupled (blockchain and FL operations separated via committee selection or smart contracts), and loosely coupled (reputation-based, focusing on model-update validation and reputation on the ledger); and (2) a generic bottom-up 6-layer BCFL system architecture (infrastructure, network, communication, algorithm, blockchain consensus, and application layers) intended as both an evaluative tool for existing systems and a design guide for new ones. The paper positions itself against prior BCFL reviews (e.g., refs [6], [95], [49], [34]) by claiming to amalgamate their distinct, partial views into a single unified analysis, and closes with open research directions (data security/privacy, model efficiency, scalability).
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not define a single adversary but catalogs the threat space that motivates integrating blockchain into FL. Core assumptions it surfaces: the FL aggregator/central server is a single point of failure and may be honest-but-curious or actively malicious ('aggregator may behave dishonestly'); clients may be malicious and upload poisonous/biased model updates (model poisoning). FL-level privacy threats it enumerates: data leakage / reconstruction from shared model parameters, and membership inference attacks (inferring whether a data point was in the training set). Blockchain's role in the paper's framing is to remove the trusted central aggregator, provide immutable/traceable audit of models and data provenance, enable reputation-based exclusion of malicious actors, and support incentive/accountability mechanisms; privacy is handled by complementary techniques (differential privacy, homomorphic encryption, secure multi-party computation, TEEs) layered on top. No formal security proofs or quantified attack/defense evaluations are the paper's own.
limitations: As a survey it presents no experiments, datasets, or head-to-head measurements, so all performance/security claims are inherited from cited works and are not independently validated. The taxonomy and 6-layer architecture are conceptual organizing devices, not empirically evaluated. Coverage is qualitative and somewhat descriptive (many systems summarized in one or two sentences) with no quantitative comparison table across the surveyed BCFL systems, making it hard to judge relative effectiveness. Open-direction discussion (efficiency, scalability, privacy) is high-level. The paper also does not systematically distinguish which surveyed systems have been deployed vs. proposed only in prototype/simulation.

baselines_compared: Positions itself (qualitatively, not experimentally) against prior BCFL surveys/reviews: blockchain-for-information-systems survey [6]; FL-enhancement-via-blockchain review [95]; systemic BCFL survey [49]; and comprehensive BCFL-for-IoT-security survey [34]

Representative surveyed BCFL systems it catalogs (cited work, NOT the paper's own results): BLADE-FL [75], DeepChain [119], FedCoin [67], Biscotti [102], TrustFed [111], RepBFL [15], BFLC (committee consensus) [57], Refiner [136], SPDL [121], Proof of Federated Training/PoFT [12], BML-ES [107], LearningChain [17], Swarm Learning [118]
